data.title: Expect still 3 rate cuts amid ‘murky’ economic data – HSBC’s James Pomeroy
data.description:
James Pomeroy, Global Economist at HSBC, still expects three interest rate cuts from the U.S. Federal Reserve as economic data continues to send mixed signals.

During a CNBC interview, Pomeroy said he expects the cuts to happen in September, December, and March, as he cited concerns about weakness in the U.S. labor market and consumer spending.

“It’s been a year where I think…it’s been really, really hard to get a handle on exactly what’s happening,” he said, pointing to distortions in GDP growth data across various countries due to front-loading of trade flows, creating what he called a “very murky picture” that makes economic analysis particularly difficult.

The economist explained that artificial enhancement of U.S. economic data has occurred as businesses rushed imports ahead of potential tariff implementations.

This pattern has “juiced up a lot of the data in the first half of this year,” according to Pomeroy, and will likely lead to economic payback in subsequent months, potentially weighing on growth worldwide.

Beyond the U.S., Pomeroy indicated that more rate cuts are likely in various global economies.

“We think there’s room opening up for a few more rate cuts in Asia as well and potentially around that lower inflation story,” he noted, adding that the European Central Bank is probably done at 2%, after already delivering significant easing.

Business uncertainty remains a critical concern affecting global economic performance, as companies struggle with planning. “If you’re a business, then what do you do in this environment?” Pomeroy asked rhetorically, explaining that many businesses are responding by pausing hiring and investment decisions, which means “global growth under the surface is probably running a lot slower than the headline numbers thing.”

More on the U.S. Economy:

  • July's nonfarm payrolls report shows deteriorating labor market, boosting odds of September rate cut
  • Did The Fed Really Deliver A Hawkish Hold? The FX Market Seems To Think So
  • New tariff rates mean even more revenue for U.S. Treasury
  • Fed's preferred inflation gauge comes in a touch hotter than expected Y/Y
  • July U.S. job cuts jump 29% from June: Challenger Job Cut report

data.title: Baytex outlines commercialization plan targeting 20,000–25,000 BOE/d by 2029 amid Duvernay efficiency gains
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Baytex Energy Corp. (BTE) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Eric Thomas Greager, President and CEO, stated that Baytex delivered "solid operational and financial results in the second quarter that reflect the quality of our assets as well as our focus on operational excellence." He highlighted achieving the highest 30-day peak oil rates in the Pembina Duvernay and noted a 7% quarter-over-quarter growth in heavy oil production. Greager said, "In this volatile environment, we remain focused on capital discipline, prioritizing free cash flow and reducing net debt."
  • CFO Chad L. Kalmakoff reported, "Adjusted funds flow was $367 million or $0.48 per basic share, and we generated net income of $152 million. We generated $3 million in free cash flow and returned $21 million to shareholders, including $4 million in share repurchases and $17 million in quarterly dividends. Net debt decreased $96 million or 4% to $2.3 billion, supported by a strengthening Canadian dollar."
  • COO Chad E. Lundberg emphasized operational execution, with production averaging 148,095 BOE per day and a 2% production per share increase year-over-year. He stated, "The performance of our first 2 pads has exceeded initial rate expectations. With the first pad delivering the highest 30-day peak oil rates to date in the West Shale Basin."

Outlook

  • CEO Greager projected, "Based on forward strip pricing, we expect to generate approximately $400 million of free cash flow in 2025 with the majority weighted to the second half of the year given our production and capital spending profile."
  • Management plans to allocate 100% of free cash flow to debt repayment after funding quarterly dividend payments, targeting net debt of approximately $2 billion by year-end. Greager added, "Looking ahead, our oil-weighted production profile provides significant exposure to oil price upside with approximately 84% of our production weighted towards crude oil and liquids."
  • Lundberg outlined a commercialization plan for the Duvernay, with a transition to drilling 18 to 20 wells per year and production ramping to 20,000 to 25,000 BOE per day by 2029–2030.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted funds flow for the quarter was $367 million, net income reached $152 million, and free cash flow totaled $3 million. The company returned $21 million to shareholders and reduced net debt by $96 million to $2.3 billion. USD 41 million of 8.5% long-term notes were repurchased. Baytex maintains USD 1.1 billion in credit facility capacity, less than 25% drawn, maturing in June 2029, with the earliest note maturity in April 2030.
  • Production averaged 148,095 BOE per day, with exploration and development expenditures of $357 million and 67 wells brought on stream.

Q&A

  • Laique Ahmad Amir Arif, ATB Capital: Asked about average well cost in the Duvernay. Greager responded, "The average well cost so far this year has been running right at $12.5 million. So for a 12,000-foot lateral, a 12,500-foot lateral, that's right at $1,000 per completed lateral foot."
  • Arif: Inquired about commercialization drilling plans. Greager explained, "In 2026, we're targeting 12 to 15 wells. It kind of depends on the balance of the year and kind of commodity price... But we're shooting for 12 to 15, and that continues to step toward full commercialization."
  • Arif: Asked about Eagle Ford refrac decline rates. Greager stated, "It's a little bit too early on the 2 refracs in 2025 to know really with data specificity around decline rates. But so far, so good."
  • Arif: Queried on cost improvements in Eagle Ford. Lundberg attributed the 11% improvement to "a combination of...service cost reductions" and "continued efficiency gains," also noting, "efficiencies are sticky, and that's how we get more excited about them because they last through all parts of the commodity cycle."
  • Webcast question: On variability in Duvernay well performance. Lundberg described consistent performance across wells, with differences attributed to "rock characteristic differences, reservoir characteristic differences," and some early-stage facility trials.
  • Online question: On infrastructure spending in the Duvernay, Lundberg shared, "We think about it as $25 million to $30 million a year for these early years, liberating itself to a lower rate in the out years."
  • Webcast question: On Eagle Ford refrac capital allocation, Greager said, "We've got 300 opportunities identified in our current base, and we intend to step up the pace of our refracs, bringing those into the program with greater frequency."
  • Online question: On nonoperating Eagle Ford asset post-Conoco acquisition. Greager said, "We believe that we've got a strong relationship, and we believe that the development is going to continue moving forward, and we're very comfortable with the plans that we've seen."
  • Webcast question: On hedging strategy, Kalmakoff responded, "We're fairly hedged here in 2025. On the oil side, we've been targeting $60 floors and then selling calls on top of that to kind of fund the puts where we can."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions about cost improvements, drilling plans, and refrac performance, with a tone of curiosity and cautious optimism.
  • Management maintained a confident and positive tone, frequently referencing operational achievements and efficiency gains. Greager used phrases like "very encouraged by the opportunity for this commercialization" and "couldn't be more excited" regarding refrac results.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts appeared more optimistic, shifting from a defensive posture focused on capital discipline and debt reduction to highlighting operational outperformance and strategic advancement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter emphasized Duvernay commercialization and significant cost improvements, while the previous quarter focused on capital discipline and navigating a challenging commodity environment.
  • Guidance shifted from expecting $200 million of free cash flow (Q1) to $400 million (Q2), with a target to reduce net debt further by year-end.
  • Management's tone evolved from defensive and cautious in Q1 to more confident and forward-looking in Q2, especially regarding Duvernay and Eagle Ford asset performance.
  • Analysts' concerns moved from capital allocation and hedging to operational specifics and efficiency gains, reflecting improved sentiment.
  • Strategic focus shifted towards asset commercialization and operational scaling, particularly in the Duvernay.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted a "soft" commodity backdrop in Q2, with WTI averaging USD 64 per barrel.
  • Exposure to oil price volatility remains a key risk, with sensitivity of approximately $225 million in annual adjusted funds flow for every USD $5 per barrel change in WTI.
  • The company continues to monitor decline rates post-refracs, with early data not yet fully available.
  • Infrastructure spending in the Duvernay is front-end loaded, with $25–$30 million per year anticipated, but longer-term needs expected to decline.
  • Analysts probed for variability in Duvernay well performance and the sustainability of cost reductions, indicating ongoing scrutiny around operational consistency and capital efficiency.

Final Takeaway

Baytex Energy Corp.'s Q2 2025 results showcased robust operational execution, notably with record-setting Duvernay well performance and meaningful cost reductions. The company outlined a clear path toward Duvernay commercialization, targeting 18 to 20 wells per year and a production ramp to 20,000–25,000 BOE per day by 2029–2030. With free cash flow guidance raised to $400 million and a firm commitment to debt reduction, management expressed confidence in navigating market volatility while enhancing shareholder value through operational efficiencies and strategic asset development.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Fed starts to tackle new proposal for Basel III endgame - report
data.description:
The Federal Reserve is rebooting its process for establishing a new risk-based capital rule that would lower the burden on the largest U.S. banks compared with a Biden-era plan, according to a media report on Friday.

Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman is leading the effort to design a new proposal that's intended to simplify the way banks calculate their capital requirements, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter. The regulators are expected to throw out the original 1,087-page Basel III endgame proposal issued in 2023 and aim to introduce a new one as soon as Q1 2026, the people said.

The Basel III endgame rules are intended to show banks how much capital they need to set aside to weather economic downturns. Former Fed Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr's first proposal for the rules would have resulted in a 19% increase in capital requirements for the biggest U.S. banks. He later floated a revised version that would have pared the increase to 9%. Once Donald Trump was elected president in November 2024, the process to finalize the rule ground to a halt.

The Federal Reserve will be developing the new rules alongside the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, the other major U.S. bank regulators. The yet-to-be drafted proposal is expected to result in a lower increase than the revised version's 9% figure, Bloomberg News said.

The process is starting after bankers and lobbyists gathered in Washington, DC, for a conference hosted by the Fed — Integrated Review of the Capital Framework for Large Banks Conference — to provide their input before tackling the new rule.

Bowman was a sharp critic of the original Basel III endgame rule proposal, saying it risked "over-calibrating" and was even stricter than the Basel standards set by the Bank for International Settlements.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Johnson Outdoors signals further operational efficiencies and innovation focus as sales rise 5%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Johnson Outdoors Inc. (JOUT) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Helen P. Johnson-Leipold, Chairman & CEO, reported that "sales in our third fiscal quarter ending June 2025 increased 5% to $180.7 million compared to $172.5 million in the prior year third quarter." She highlighted that operating profit was up significantly to $7.3 million versus an operating loss in the previous third quarter. Johnson-Leipold emphasized the strong performance of the Fishing business, stating that demand for Hummingbird's new MEGA Live 2 Sonar and the new XPLORE fish finder unit "has been exceeding expectations." She announced that the XPLORE series captured Best in Electronics honors at ICAST and mentioned the Marine Power Innovation Award for the Minn Kota brand. The Camping and Watercraft segment saw strong demand for Jetboil's newest Fast Boil cooking systems, and Old Town continued to lead despite a weak marketplace. The Diving business showed slight improvement with ongoing integration work on a recently acquired supplier.
  • Johnson-Leipold also detailed the company's ongoing focus on innovation, operational efficiencies, and e-commerce, supported by a debt-free balance sheet and a strong cash position, asserting, "We are confident these are the right things to position us for future healthy profitable growth."
  • David W. Johnson, CFO, stated, "Profit before income taxes in the third quarter was up significantly due to an increase in sales, gross margin improvements and a reduction in operating expenses versus the prior year." He reported that gross margin in the third quarter was 37.6%, up 1.8 points from last year's quarter, and operating expenses decreased $1.7 million versus the prior year third quarter. Johnson added that inventory balance as of June was $163.7 million, down about $59.4 million from last year's third quarter and down from fiscal year-end.

Outlook

  • Management indicated that while the watercraft and diving markets remain challenged, the company continues to invest in innovation and operational efficiencies. Johnson-Leipold expressed confidence in the current strategic direction and noted, "We continue to invest and execute on our strategic priorities, innovation, operational efficiencies and e-commerce and our resiliency as a company is further bolstered by our debt-free balance sheet and cash position."
  • Johnson commented on tariffs, explaining, "I do expect more cost coming in, in the fourth quarter. So we're forecasting that to see the tariffs kind of flow through our inventories and get out to be expensed."

Financial Results

  • The company reported sales of $180.7 million for the third quarter. Gross margin was 37.6%, an increase of 1.8 points from the prior year quarter, and operating expenses decreased by $1.7 million year-over-year. Inventory balance as of June was $163.7 million.
  • CFO Johnson noted cost savings from lower promotion and professional services expenses and highlighted continued progress on inventory levels, saying, "Our inventory balance as of June was $163.7 million, down about $59.4 million from last year's third quarter and down from our fiscal year-end."

Q&A

  • Anthony Chester Lebiedzinski, Sidoti & Company: "Can you comment on the cadence of sales that you saw throughout the quarter as you went from April through June? And can you give us maybe some early read or color as to how the month of July was?" CEO Johnson-Leipold responded, "Every month, we saw improvement in our sales. So it was a positive trend. It's a little early to talk about the final quarter, but it is towards the end of the season, and we are hoping to end in a good spot looking forward to more stability certainly for next season."
  • Lebiedzinski: "How should we think about the impact of tariffs going forward?" CFO Johnson replied, "I do expect more cost coming in, in the fourth quarter. So we're forecasting that to see the tariffs kind of flow through our inventories and get out to be expensed."
  • Lebiedzinski: "Can you comment on pricing actions?" CFO Johnson stated, "We have taken pricing on certain product lines in our portfolio. We'll continue to look at that going forward kind of across the portfolio, keeping in mind the consumer dynamics that we're looking at, competitive situation, et cetera."
  • Lebiedzinski: "Can you give us an update on the cost savings program?" CFO Johnson said, "The cost savings program is robust. We last year had a ton of factory efficiencies that we recognized. We continue that work this year, and we are focused on product cost improvements. And that will take a little bit of time to manifest, but we're very excited about the portfolio of initiatives that we've got in the pipeline."
  • Lebiedzinski: "Do you think you can sustain this type of reduced promotional activity going forward?" CEO Johnson-Leipold explained, "We obviously are in very competitive markets. And we put programs together to reflect what we need to do. ... we will always have to consider promotions as a tactic depending on what's going on in the market."
  • Lebiedzinski: "Have you seen a further uptick in demand because of the ICAST award?" CEO Johnson-Leipold responded, "It just adds to the momentum. So we were very happy about that. But it definitely got off to a very good start."
  • Lebiedzinski: "Do you think you can further reduce inventory in the next couple of quarters?" CFO Johnson replied, "We're working on that for sure. I think there's more improvement to be had. ... But we feel good about where we are right now, but we know we got more progress to make."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive tone, frequently congratulating management on improved sales and profitability and expressing interest in the sustainability of positive trends, while pressing for details on tariffs and cost controls.
  • Management's tone was confident and cautiously optimistic during prepared remarks, reinforcing their commitment to innovation and operational discipline. In the Q&A, management remained open and constructive, with Johnson-Leipold stating "we are hoping to end in a good spot looking forward to more stability certainly for next season."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts showed a shift toward more optimism, moving from cautious concern about a tough market to a tone celebrating improvement and the impact of innovation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a notable improvement in sales and profitability compared to the prior quarter's cautious outlook, with operating profit turning positive and gross margin increasing.
  • Management's discussion shifted from emphasizing ongoing market difficulties and the need for resilience to highlighting the success of recent product launches and awards, as well as improved financial results.
  • Analysts' questions evolved from focusing on survival strategies and tariff risks to exploring the sustainability of recent positive trends and the impact of industry recognition on demand.
  • Management's confidence in its innovation strategy and operational focus was more pronounced, with specific examples of awards and sales momentum.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified ongoing macroeconomic challenges, market fluctuations, and tariff-related cost pressures as continuing uncertainties.
  • Johnson-Leipold noted the weak watercraft marketplace and challenges in diving, but expressed confidence in the company's positioning and integration efforts.
  • CFO Johnson acknowledged that tariffs will continue to add costs in the next quarter and that strategies to mitigate these will evolve as the environment changes.

Final Takeaway

Johnson Outdoors Inc. delivered improved sales and returned to profitability in the third quarter of 2025, driven by strong demand for innovative products, successful cost controls, and enhanced operational efficiency. Management remains focused on navigating macroeconomic uncertainties, addressing tariff impacts with pricing and supply chain strategies, and building on recent momentum in key business segments. The outlook is supported by a robust cash position, a debt-free balance sheet, and ongoing investments in innovation—all positioning the company to pursue healthy, profitable growth in the quarters ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Eversource reaffirms 2025 EPS guidance of $4.67–$4.82 as infrastructure investments and regulatory progress advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eversource Energy (ES) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Joseph R. Nolan, President, CEO & Chairman, reported "great progress halfway through the year, executing our key strategic priorities while maintaining our commitment to being a pure-play pipes and wires regulated utility." Management noted that electric demand growth through the first half of 2025 exceeded 2%, nearly double the rate from the same period last year.
  • The company announced a "10% increase in our 5-year infrastructure investment plan that we announced in February." The balance sheet is reportedly strengthening, with improvements to the FFO to debt ratio due to constructive regulatory outcomes and cash flow enhancements, including exiting the offshore wind business and the planned divestiture of the water business.
  • Nolan stated, "earnings for the second quarter were $0.96 a share, in line with our expectations. We are reaffirming our 2025 EPS guidance range of $4.67 per share to $4.82 per share as well as our long-term EPS growth projection of 5% to 7% through 2029."
  • The advanced metering infrastructure rollout in Massachusetts reached a milestone with the AMI communication network in Western Massachusetts "substantially complete" and meter installation underway. The AMI initiative for all Massachusetts electric customers is expected to take approximately three years.
  • Construction of the Cambridge Underground substation is progressing, with Boston Properties advancing toward the final depth. The Outer Cape battery energy storage system was recognized for its technical achievement and reliability improvement.
  • On the regulatory front, Nolan highlighted the passage of Connecticut Senate Bill 4, which allows for securitization of storm costs and clarifies regulatory requirements. The Aquarion water business divestiture process is underway, with closure expected by year-end, and a favorable Connecticut Supreme Court ruling clarified prudency standards for future investments.
  • John M. Moreira, EVP, CFO & Treasurer, reported "GAAP and recurring earnings results for the second quarter were $0.96 per share compared with GAAP and recurring earnings of $0.95 per share last year. Higher utility earnings were largely offset by a decrease in parent and other earnings."

Outlook

  • Eversource reaffirmed its 2025 recurring EPS guidance range of $4.67 to $4.82 and a long-term EPS growth rate of 5% to 7% through 2029.
  • The company maintains its five-year capital plan of $24.2 billion, reflecting a 10% increase over the previous plan, and sees additional capital investment opportunities of $1.5 billion to $2 billion within this period.
  • Management indicated that completion of the Aquarion divestiture and progress on storm cost securitization could impact equity needs and financial flexibility, with further updates expected in February.

Financial Results

  • Eversource reported second quarter GAAP and recurring earnings of $0.96 per share.
  • Transmission earnings increased due to higher revenues from continued investment and lower interest expense, partially offset by share dilution.
  • Electric distribution earnings rose, benefiting from rate increases in New Hampshire and Massachusetts, though offset by higher property taxes, interest, and depreciation.
  • Natural gas segment earnings improved due to base distribution rate increases at Massachusetts utilities, offset by higher expenses and share dilution. Water distribution earnings also improved.
  • Parent losses increased primarily due to higher interest expense following the offshore wind business sale.
  • Operating cash flows increased over $1 billion year-over-year for the first half of 2025, attributed to executed cash flow enhancements and constructive regulatory outcomes.

Q&A

  • Carly S. Davenport, Goldman Sachs, asked about confidence in reaching a 14% FFO to debt level by year-end. Moreira responded with "very highly confident" and explained the drivers, including recovery of deferrals and the Aquarion closing contributing about 100 basis points.
  • Davenport also asked about the impact and timing of storm cost securitization. Moreira said, "within 6 months, we're going to do a clean refresh," with a process timeline likely pushing potential cash inflow to 2027.
  • Jeremy Bryan Tonet, JPMorgan, asked for updated FFO to debt metrics and progress. Moreira stated, "every quarter, we'll continue to make progress," aiming for 13% in coming quarters.
  • Tonet also inquired about regulatory process takeaways from New Hampshire. Nolan described it as "very constructive, not only with the commission, but with all of the parties that were involved."
  • Andrew Marc Weisel, Scotiabank, asked about ATM equity issuance and future needs. Moreira explained that future equity raises will depend on Aquarion approval and short-term balances, stating, "Once we close Aquarion, I don't see the need to raise any equity for some time."
  • Weisel also asked about redeploying capital to Connecticut following legislative and court developments. Moreira replied, "We would like to see some more constructive data points coming out of the commission before we reassess our capital redeployment."
  • Agnieszka Anna Storozynski, Seaport, asked about equity needs and CapEx. Moreira affirmed a February refresh would clarify equity needs, factoring in Aquarion and potential additional capital investments.
  • Ryan Michael Levine, Citigroup, asked about prudency standards’ impact. Moreira said the court clarified that "the commission cannot apply hindsight in the rate recovery process," with Nolan adding, "I think it has changed. I think it's improved."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the company’s balance sheet strength, FFO to debt ratios, regulatory progress, and equity needs, with a tone that was neutral to slightly positive, seeking clarification on financial drivers and regulatory developments.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with Moreira repeatedly expressing high confidence in meeting financial targets and regulatory recovery timelines. Nolan emphasized constructive regulatory outcomes and operational excellence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remained steady, with continued attention to regulatory clarity and capital needs. Management’s tone was consistent, with ongoing confidence in execution and progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 EPS was reaffirmed, with no change from the previous quarter’s range.
  • Strategic focus remained on infrastructure investment, reliability, regulatory clarity, and balance sheet improvement.
  • The biggest shift was the reported progress on AMI deployment, further regulatory clarity in Connecticut, and an update on the Aquarion divestiture timeline.
  • Analysts maintained their focus on capital allocation, equity issuance timing, and regulatory impacts, while management sustained a confident outlook and addressed balance sheet strategies in greater detail.

Risks and Concerns

  • The company identified regulatory uncertainty in Connecticut as a challenge, particularly regarding storm cost recovery and rate case outcomes.
  • Management discussed the timeline and process risk in securing storm cost securitization, noting that significant cash inflow from this is now expected in 2027 rather than 2026.
  • Moody’s downgrade of Connecticut Light & Power to Baa1 was cited, with management attributing it to the Connecticut regulatory environment.
  • Analysts raised concerns about equity needs, capital redeployment, and the impact of regulatory decisions in Connecticut and New Hampshire.

Final Takeaway

Eversource management emphasized that the company is executing on key infrastructure investments and regulatory milestones, supporting reaffirmed 2025 EPS guidance and a long-term growth outlook. The successful progress in AMI rollout, constructive regulatory decisions, and anticipated closure of the Aquarion divestiture are expected to drive further balance sheet improvement and operational strength, while management remains vigilant to evolving regulatory risks and capital needs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: AMD, TSLA, SHEL, PTCT
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Tesla (TSLA) have been downgraded by Seeking Alpha analysts due to valuation concerns, while Shell (SHEL) receives an upgrade on improved production outlooks. PTC Therapeutics (PTCT) also sees an upgrade to Hold from Sell, reflecting improved prospects despite ongoing uncertainties around key regulatory decisions.

Upgrades

  • Shell plc (SHEL): Upgrade to Buy by Manika Premsingh. Despite weak Q2 2025 results, the analyst cites improved production forecasts and higher expected gas prices that will positively impact the company’s financials.

    “If production held Shell back in Q2 2025, however, it can give it a push forward in the next quarter as well. The guidance certainly suggests so… There’s a lot more going for Shell now compared to where it was the last I checked. Despite the drop in earnings, the company’s dividend payout ratio continues to look alright at 43.7%.”

  • PTC Therapeutics, Inc. (PTCT): Upgrade to Hold by Edmund Ingham. Despite ongoing uncertainties about FDA decisions for Translarna and Vatiquinone, the company’s strengthened cash position from the Novartis partnership and potential product approvals present a more balanced risk-reward profile.

    “PTC is armed with >$2bn cash and note that losses are narrowing… There is a significant amount of uncertainty in play, in my view, but enough evidence to support an upgrade to ‘Hold.’ I will have to leave readers to draw their own conclusions about whether to sell stock or buy it ahead of what promises to be a momentous second half of 2025 for the company.”

Downgrades

  • Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD): Downgrade by Oliver Rodzianko. The analyst closed his entire AMD position, suggesting the stock’s valuation is too high ahead of Q2 earnings, with a recommendation to trim positions due to expected market corrections.

    “AMD stock is currently trading at a 14-week RSI value of 73, and the stock price is now significantly higher than the 50-week moving average in price. This is really a very obvious moment to trim your AMD stock… It’s best not to be greedy—those are world-class returns and sentiment-value investors like myself know that the massive AMD value opportunity is over and was prime in March following an extended downtrend.”

  • Tesla, Inc. (TSLA): Double-downgrade from Strong Buy to Hold by Noah’s Arc Capital Management. The analyst cites Musk’s distractions and operational risks affecting Tesla’s growth narrative, with consecutive delivery declines undermining the company’s growth story.

    “I strongly believe that Tesla is distracted and their key tech (FSD) is falling behind. I don’t think it’s a coincidence that these efforts coincide with declining delivery numbers… While Q2 earnings were a slight beat, one of the biggest worries I have is the stark disconnect between the ~35% rally from early April and the ~13% drop in H1 2025 vehicle deliveries compared to last year.”

data.title: Apple still has time to strengthen its AI race – Janus Henderson’s Richard Clode
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) faces significant challenges despite posting impressive quarterly numbers, said Richard Clode, Janus Henderson Investors portfolio manager.

In an interview with CNBC, Clode noted that Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) and other major tech companies have beaten expectations and received positive earnings revisions, yet their stock performance has been disappointing. Apple’s shares – -2.2% on Friday – have dropped nearly 19% year-to-date, reflecting investor concerns about the company’s future.

“Tim Cook and team probably feel a little bit sore today after putting up those numbers,” Clode said.

He identified several factors weighing on Apple’s stock (AAPL), including the impact of tariffs that could significantly affect the company’s cost structure and pricing strategy. Clode specifically mentioned $1B of tariff costs that don’t even include upcoming Section 232 tariffs.

The technology investor also pointed to the Department of Justice ruling with Alphabet (GOOG), (GOOGL) coming in early September as a potential threat to Apple’s (AAPL) strong services growth. Additionally, Apple’s perceived lag in artificial intelligence development compared to competitors has created uncertainty about the company’s future direction. “The market’s very much debating what is this company’s future in an AI world, and very much seen as a laggard.”

Despite these challenges, Clode believes Apple (AAPL) still has time to strengthen its AI strategy. He referenced Apple’s approach to innovation, paraphrasing Tim Cook’s philosophy that Apple isn’t always first to market but ultimately delivers better products.

Clode suggested that Apple (AAPL) could pursue more aggressive capital expenditure and potential M&A activities, possibly even exploring partnerships with companies like Alphabet once regulatory issues are resolved.

He also warned that Apple needs to demonstrate progress in its AI initiatives within the next year to avoid further market devaluation.

“If we’re in the same place a year ahead from here, we go to another WWDC (Apple's Worldwide Developers Conference) without too much innovation, the market’s going to get more worried and keep derating the company,” he cautioned.

data.title: Colgate-Palmolive outlines $200M–$300M productivity initiative as it advances 2030 strategic plan
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Colgate-Palmolive (CL) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Noel Wallace stated that Colgate-Palmolive "grew net sales, organic sales and earnings per share despite significant raw material pressure and negative foreign exchange" in Q2. Excluding lower private label, organic sales growth accelerated to 2.4% with slightly positive volume, particularly due to improvement in North America and Africa/Eurasia.
  • Wallace announced the closing of the acquisition of Prime100, the top vet-recommended fresh pet food brand in Australia, and noted "significant innovation across categories, geographies and price tiers" in the quarter.
  • The company launched a productivity initiative, with Wallace stating, "Today, we also announced a productivity initiative that is focused on prioritizing incremental investment and accelerating our capabilities to build a more future-fit organization as we transition to our 2030 strategic plan."
  • Wallace highlighted strategic adjustments in response to consumer value-seeking behavior, leveraging "price pack architecture" and the company's global supply chain to address changing preferences.
  • CFO Stanley Sutula said, "We've announced a productivity program here that will be $200 million to $300 million of a charge over a 3-year period...including optimizing our supply chain and looking at where we need to make key strategic shifts."

Outlook

  • Management guided that gross margin will be "roughly flat for 2025" despite higher raw material costs and lower organic sales, with Wallace noting, "we feel very good about the guidance that we provided on the call this morning."
  • Sutula reiterated, "you shouldn't assume any major impact here in the back half of the year" from the productivity initiative, as execution and investment will occur over a 2–3 year period.
  • Wallace commented, "we expect [categories] to get modestly better, but not significantly better," with guidance reflecting this cautious optimism.

Financial Results

  • Wallace reported "improvement in North America" and that "market share has improved during the quarter."
  • Sutula detailed that "gross margin was down year-over-year in the quarter, driven by a combination of greater anticipated raw material inflation and tariffs...Q2 '24 was our lowest level of material inflation...140 basis points versus 420 basis points this year."
  • Sutula added that gross profit for the first half is "flat year-to-year."
  • Wallace said, "Hill's ex private label...was 5% organic, consistent with where we were in the first quarter...volume was 2% and price was 3%."

Q&A

  • Dara Warren Mohsenian, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the restructuring program and U.S. category growth. Wallace responded the productivity initiative is intended "to accelerate all of the things we want to do" for the 2030 plan, focusing on capability building and innovation. Sutula explained savings will be in the same range as previous initiatives and is "going to be done thoughtfully and done with the right opportunity to drive our structural changes."
  • Robert Edward Ottenstein, Evercore ISI: Asked about the Colgate Total relaunch and Latin America. Wallace said, "big core relaunch for us out of the gates, quite strong in Latin America...encouraged by what we're seeing" and noted that innovation and pricing in the region will benefit the back half of the year despite some deceleration in Brazil.
  • Filippo Falorni, Citi: Inquired about gross margin outlook. Sutula clarified, "gross margin guidance stays roughly the same...lower tariff exposure, offset by higher raw material costs and lower organic sales."
  • Bonnie Lee Herzog, Goldman Sachs: Sought clarity on EPS guidance. Wallace remarked, "we feel very good about the guidance that we provided on the call this morning," with Sutula adding confidence comes from the team's ability to manage productivity and expenses.
  • Peter Thomas Galbo, BofA Securities: Asked about Hill's private label exit. Sutula answered, "we stopped producing private label as of July...the impact will be closer to 80 or 90 basis points" in 3Q and 4Q year-on-year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on the productivity initiative, gross margin outlook, and regional performance, with a generally neutral to slightly cautious tone, especially regarding macroeconomic uncertainty, category growth, and the restructuring program.
  • Management consistently projected confidence, as seen in Wallace's remarks: "we feel very good about the guidance," though some hesitation was present regarding macroeconomic and consumer uncertainty.
  • Compared to Q1, the management tone remained steady, emphasizing resilience and capability-building, while analysts maintained a cautious stance amid persistent external pressures.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q2 call introduced the $200–$300 million productivity initiative tied to the 2030 strategic plan, which was not present in Q1. Guidance language about category improvement remained cautious, but the company now expects "modestly better" categories rather than a significant rebound.
  • Market share improvement in North America was highlighted in Q2, whereas Q1 focused on early signs of stabilization.
  • Hill's performance remained strong, with the exit of private label now completed.
  • Management tone continued to stress flexibility and resilience, while analysts sustained their focus on macro risks, margin headwinds, and execution of new programs.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing raw material and packaging cost pressures, tariff increases, and lower category inflation as major challenges.
  • Wallace noted "persistently cautious consumer in North America," category volatility, and geopolitical uncertainty.
  • Analysts highlighted concerns about the timing and realization of productivity savings, the impact of raw material inflation, and effectiveness of restructuring measures.
  • Management is leveraging revenue growth management, supply chain optimization, and innovation to mitigate risks but acknowledged that "things could go materially different if the categories go into further decline and we see heightened tariffs and raw material prices continue to move north."

Final Takeaway

Colgate-Palmolive emphasized resilience amid challenging conditions in Q2 2025, launching a $200–$300 million productivity program to accelerate capabilities for the 2030 strategic plan. Management reported improved market share, steady innovation, and a successful acquisition in pet food, while cautioning on persistent cost and category pressures. The company remains focused on driving organic growth and brand health, with guidance reflecting prudent optimism for modest improvement in the second half of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump privately backed marijuana rescheduling since taking office: Scotts Miracle-Gro CEO
data.description:
Citing his conversation with Donald Trump on cannabis reforms, Scotts Miracle-Gro (NYSE:SMG) CEO James Hagedorn said that the U.S. President, since his inauguration in January, has privately committed multiple times to advance a major proposal to reschedule marijuana in the U.S.

The proposal made by the Department of Justice during the Biden administration to reclassify cannabis in a low-risk category under the Controlled Substances Act (CSA) is currently undergoing review at the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration.

In an interview with Fox Business on Thursday, the CEO of the gardening supply company said that he had multiple conversations with Trump regarding cannabis policy reforms since the President took office for his second term in January.

Hagedorn added that Trump has assured him and others that he still plans to support cannabis reforms.

Commenting on newly named DEA administrator Terrance Cole’s decision not to include cannabis rescheduling as a priority for the agency after his confirmation, Hagedorn said, “Well, I think he’s a career law enforcement guy. I’m not surprised by it.”

“I think there’s one person who could change it, and he’s told me and others that he will—and that’s the president of the United States, Donald Trump, who I’m a major fan of,” he added.

data.title: Fulgent Genetics lifts 2025 revenue outlook to $320M and narrows EPS loss guidance while advancing clinical pipeline
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Fulgent Genetics (FLGT) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Ming Hsieh, CEO, stated that "We are pleased with our second quarter results. We have shown both sequential and year-over-year growth in laboratory services." He highlighted momentum in 2025 and progress in the therapeutic development pipeline, with FID-007 advancing through Phase II clinical trials and FID-022 beginning Phase I trials. He shared, "Our anticipated cost for these programs is very reasonable, and we believe our investment will be rewarded."
  • Hsieh emphasized the company’s strong financial position and ongoing strategic execution, expressing confidence in both the laboratory services and therapeutics segments.
  • Brandon Perthuis, Chief Commercial Officer, reported "delivering nearly $81.7 million in core revenue which is up 16% year-over-year and 11% sequentially" and noted sequential growth in all business lines. New product introductions included an enhanced whole genome sequencing service and the receipt of a certified CE Mark for Fulgent exome and Pipeline Manager, enabling broader European market access. Perthuis remarked, "With the CE mark, we can now make Fulgent exome available to clinics and hospital systems throughout Europe."
  • Perthuis described over 20 new managed care agreements since January, adding 35 million covered lives to in-network contracts, and signaled a continued focus on market share and sales expansion for the second half of the year.
  • Paul Kim, CFO, reported, "Revenue in the second quarter of 2025 totaled $81.8 million compared to $73.5 million in the first quarter of 2025." Kim highlighted improved gross margins, increased operating expenses due to R&D and sales investments, and noted a one-time noncash charge of $9.9 million. He shared, "On a non-GAAP basis and excluding equity-based compensation expense, intangible asset amortization and impairment loss income for the quarter was approximately $2.1 million or a positive $0.07 per share on 30.7 million weighted average diluted shares outstanding."
  • Kim announced the July acquisition of ANP Technologies for approximately $4 million, providing full control over proprietary nano drug delivery technology supporting FID-007 and FID-022.

Outlook

  • Kim stated, "We’re adjusting our revenue outlook for the remainder of the year increasing revenue guidance from $310 million to $320 million for 2025, representing a growth of 14% year-over-year."
  • He confirmed expectations for non-GAAP gross margins for the full year to slightly exceed 40% and improved non-GAAP operating margins from minus 15% to minus 13% for the year.
  • Full year 2025 non-GAAP EPS guidance improved from a loss of $0.65 per share to a loss of $0.35 per share, while GAAP EPS guidance was revised to a loss of $2.10 per share from $1.95 per share.
  • Kim projected ending 2025 with approximately $770 million in cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and investments in marketable securities.

Financial Results

  • Core revenue for Q2 was $81.7 million, with overall revenue at $81.8 million, up from $73.5 million in Q1 2025.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin reached 44.2%, and GAAP gross margin was 42.1%, both improved year-over-year and sequentially, partly due to operational efficiencies and a one-time accounting adjustment.
  • Non-GAAP operating expenses totaled $43.9 million, up from $37.4 million in Q1, reflecting increased R&D and sales and marketing spend.
  • Adjusted EBITDA loss for Q2 was approximately $3 million. The company repurchased 130,000 shares for $2.2 million during the quarter.
  • The balance sheet ended with $777.5 million in cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash, and marketable securities.

Q&A

  • Lu Li, UBS: Asked about the $10 million increase in full-year guidance and the $7 million boost in Precision Diagnostics. Brandon Perthuis explained, "the VA did play some role in that... we've done a lot of different tests for the VA... there's actually some opportunity to expand that into some further areas of genetic testing," and said the Foundation Medicine partnership is strengthening but revenue was not yet material.
  • Li inquired about Anatomic Pathology (AP) growth and the possibility of one-time impacts. Perthuis responded, "There's really no one-time event in the quarter... the growth you're seeing in AP is purely organic growth... It's blocking and tackling. It's winning new deals."
  • Li asked about gross margin, noting a 44% figure and one-time elements. Kim replied, "About half of the increase was due to a onetime accounting adjustment... the other half is due to the overall strength in our business combined with the continued efficiency that we see in the operations."
  • David Westenberg, Piper Sandler: Queried AP growth and geographic expansion. Perthuis explained, "We did put new salespeople in new geographies... there's AP opportunities plentiful across the country."
  • Westenberg asked about rare disease momentum and market penetration. Perthuis highlighted the impact of updated AAP guidelines and ongoing investments in sales and product capabilities for genomic sequencing.
  • Westenberg followed up on FID-007 enrollment. Hsieh stated, "we expect to accelerate the enrollment during the second half of the 2025... we are broadening the pool," indicating positive developments in enrollment criteria.
  • Andrew Cooper, Raymond James: Asked about AP guidance and seasonality. Perthuis noted, "we do see some seasonality in the back half of the year... there's some potential upside if these pipelines come to fruition."
  • Cooper inquired about managed care wins driving the guide increase. Perthuis and Kim emphasized strong in-network contracts and improved reimbursement, which support volume and margin improvements.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on guidance drivers, AP growth sustainability, and margin improvement with a positive but probing tone, seeking clarity on organic growth and one-time items.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, using phrases like "we are pleased," "we believe our investment will be rewarded," and "we see strength in our core business." During Q&A, responses were detailed and upbeat, acknowledging both opportunities and operational discipline.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more assertive on growth prospects, while analysts were more focused on sustained drivers and upside potential.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was raised from $310 million to $320 million in revenue and the non-GAAP EPS loss narrowed to $0.35 from $0.65 per share, with GAAP EPS loss guidance widened slightly to $2.10 from $1.95 per share.
  • Management highlighted sequential and year-over-year growth in laboratory services, an improvement from Q1’s year-over-year focus, and was more explicit about key drivers behind the outlook increase.
  • Analysts shifted from questions about why the previous guide was not raised to seeking details on the magnitude and sustainability of the improvement in Q2.
  • Management sentiment was more optimistic about growth and investment payoffs, while analysts sought granularity on margins and contract impacts.
  • The acquisition of ANP Technologies in Q2 was new, representing a strategic move not discussed in Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited higher operating expenses due to expanded R&D and sales and marketing investments.
  • Kim noted a one-time $9.9 million noncash impairment charge.
  • Perthuis acknowledged some seasonality in AP growth, cautioning that Q3 and Q4 may slow but flagged potential upside.
  • Analysts probed on the sustainability of AP growth, the timing of revenue realization from major contracts, and the organic versus inorganic growth composition.

Final Takeaway

Fulgent Genetics delivered strong sequential and year-over-year core revenue growth in Q2 2025, raised its full-year guidance, and narrowed expected non-GAAP EPS losses, citing sustained momentum across laboratory services and expanding market share. The company continues to invest in its clinical pipeline, sales force, and operational efficiency, while maintaining a robust cash position and pursuing strategic acquisitions to support future growth and product development.
